--- Possible Symptoms ---
• Engine runs rough
• Hesitation or lack of power
• Increased fuel consumption
• Illuminated MIL (Malfunction Indicator Light)

--- Possible Causes ---
• Spark plugs fouled or worn
• Intake system leaks (false air)
• Fuel pressure or fuel delivery issues
• Faulty fuel injector(s)
• Faulty ignition coil(s)

--- Possible Solutions ---

Fault Code: P242F/009263

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• Malfunction Indicator Light (MIL) ON

--- Possible Causes ---
• Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) restricted/clogged
• Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) faulty

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Check Engine related Fault Codes / Problems
• Check Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) Load
• Perform Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) Service Regeneration

--- Special Notes ---
• When found inVW Crafter (2E): 2.5l CR-TDI Engine (BJJ, BJK, BJL, BJM):Depending on the Model Year and/or if the Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) has been replaced already, different Generations of the Filter may be installed. If in doubt, check the Filter Part Number. Generation 2 (DPF2) were 2E0-254-700-E and 2E0-254-700-F, while Generation 3 (DPF3) were 2E0-254-700-G/GX.Check Engine Control Module (ECM) Software Level and installed Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) Part Number/Generation. Information on the latest Software Versions can be found in TPI 2020740 (DPF2) and TPI 2020722 (DPF3).When Replacing a DPF2 with DPF3, the Engine Control Module (ECM) Software Versionmustbe updated as well. Operating a DPF2 with DPF3 Software or vice versa will damage the DPF. For Details see TPI 2015609.If the Vehicle is used mainly for Short Trip/Distance Driving (for Example as a Delivery Truck), consider applying the special Short Trip/Distance Software as described in TPI 2018140.
• Depending on the Model Year and/or if the Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) has been replaced already, different Generations of the Filter may be installed. If in doubt, check the Filter Part Number. Generation 2 (DPF2) were 2E0-254-700-E and 2E0-254-700-F, while Generation 3 (DPF3) were 2E0-254-700-G/GX.
• Check Engine Control Module (ECM) Software Level and installed Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) Part Number/Generation. Information on the latest Software Versions can be found in TPI 2020740 (DPF2) and TPI 2020722 (DPF3).
• When Replacing a DPF2 with DPF3, the Engine Control Module (ECM) Software Versionmustbe updated as well. Operating a DPF2 with DPF3 Software or vice versa will damage the DPF. For Details see TPI 2015609.
• If the Vehicle is used mainly for Short Trip/Distance Driving (for Example as a Delivery Truck), consider applying the special Short Trip/Distance Software as described in TPI 2018140.
• Depending on the Model Year and/or if the Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) has been replaced already, different Generations of the Filter may be installed. If in doubt, check the Filter Part Number. Generation 2 (DPF2) were 2E0-254-700-E and 2E0-254-700-F, while Generation 3 (DPF3) were 2E0-254-700-G/GX.
• Check Engine Control Module (ECM) Software Level and installed Diesel Particle Filter (DPF) Part Number/Generation. Information on the latest Software Versions can be found in TPI 2020740 (DPF2) and TPI 2020722 (DPF3).
• When Replacing a DPF2 with DPF3, the Engine Control Module (ECM) Software Versionmustbe updated as well. Operating a DPF2 with DPF3 Software or vice versa will damage the DPF. For Details see TPI 2015609.
• If the Vehicle is used mainly for Short Trip/Distance Driving (for Example as a Delivery Truck), consider applying the special Short Trip/Distance Software as described in TPI 2018140.

Fault Code: P246E

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• Power Loss/Limp mode
• MIL on
• Glow Plug Light on
• DPF Light on

--- Possible Causes ---
• EGT sensor faulty
• EGT wiring faulty

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Check EGT sensor and wiring.
• Note, when found in RoW B8 chassis A4 (8K) models with Engine code CAGA which uses the UDS protocol:The Advanced Measuring Values for EGT sensors may not be labeled correctly!  Make sure to check [*ENG106430*]Exhaust gas temperature before particulate filtereven though this is the last (4th) sensor.Unplugging the suspect EGT sensor while re-checking the resulting fault codes and evaluating Advanced Measuring Values would be advised before replacing parts.
• The Advanced Measuring Values for EGT sensors may not be labeled correctly!  Make sure to check [*ENG106430*]Exhaust gas temperature before particulate filtereven though this is the last (4th) sensor.
• Unplugging the suspect EGT sensor while re-checking the resulting fault codes and evaluating Advanced Measuring Values would be advised before replacing parts.
• The Advanced Measuring Values for EGT sensors may not be labeled correctly!  Make sure to check [*ENG106430*]Exhaust gas temperature before particulate filtereven though this is the last (4th) sensor.

Fault Code: P24D8

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• MIL on

--- Possible Causes ---
• EVAP Pressure Sensor (G950) faulty
• EVAP Pressure Sensor (G950)

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Check  EVAP Pressure Sensor (G950) sensor and wiring.
• Note, this fault text corresponds to what is shown in the factory scan tool but may be partially incorrect (we think it should be " Pressure Sensor" instead of "Pressure Sensor 2". For example, in a 2019 Audi A6 3.0T with the DLZA engine, there is only one EVAP pressure sensor, not two. Unplugging the sensor G950 will set this fault. Some V8 applications may have multiple sensors and it's unclear if this fault is used with those or something different exists.

Fault Code: P2533
Standard OBD-II Code: P2533

--- Special Notes ---
When found in the 4F chassis Audi A6:
• Seehttps://forums.ross-tech.com/showthread.php?6433-Case-Study-2007-A6-crank-no-start-P2533
• Check the terminal 50  (Starter solenoid signal) wire from terminal 8/87 of the J53 relay -to- T17a/16 for damage in the E-Box.  If that wire is OK make sure the starter cranks when bypassing the J53 relay before tracing the Black/Red wire to the Engine controller.
When found in any other Audi using a similar 2-relay (J53 and J695) Starter system verify the wiring diagram matches the above notes before removing the wipers and opening the E-box.

Fault Code: P2556
Standard OBD-II Code: P2556

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• MIL On
• Cooling system malfunctioning

--- Possible Causes ---
• Faulty Engine Coolant Level Sensor (G32)
• Faulty Engine Temperature Control Actuator (N493)
• Wiring or power/ground issues to those components

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Check Engine Coolant Level Sensor (G32), Engine Temperature Control Actuator (N493) in addition to the related wiring per therepair manual.

--- Special Notes ---
• At face value you would initially believe this DTC is for the Engine Coolant Level Sensor however in most cases that sensor is wired to the 17-Instrument Cluster or the 09-Central Electronics.  The Coolant Level Sensor is not usually wired directly to the 01-Engine control module.  In those cases the thermostat (N493) and the wiring to it should be checked as per post #8 in this thread:https://forums.ross-tech.com/index.php?threads/28967/The example vehicle would use this generation 09-Cent. Elect. moduleMLB based Cent. Elect. (J519) BCM1and measuring block group 028.1 can be used to check the Coolant Level (G32) status.

Fault Code: P261A/009754

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• Fault code stored in memory, no check engine light
• Coolant pump is not operating
• "In certain North American vehicles with a 2.0L TFSI gasoline engine, the electric (after run) coolant pump may short circuit and/or overheat, potentially leading to a vehicle fire"

--- Possible Causes ---
• Faulty electrical coolant pump
• Coolant pump is disconnectedIf Recall/Service Action19N4was completed, the coolant pump will be disconnected and no fault code will be present.If the above is true, then the vehicle should have an open Campaign for the19N8Service action
• If Recall/Service Action19N4was completed, the coolant pump will be disconnected and no fault code will be present.If the above is true, then the vehicle should have an open Campaign for the19N8Service action
• If the above is true, then the vehicle should have an open Campaign for the19N8Service action
• If Recall/Service Action19N4was completed, the coolant pump will be disconnected and no fault code will be present.If the above is true, then the vehicle should have an open Campaign for the19N8Service action
• If the above is true, then the vehicle should have an open Campaign for the19N8Service action
• If the above is true, then the vehicle should have an open Campaign for the19N8Service action

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Install a new electric (after-run) coolant pump and update ECM software (Audi dealer only)
• Check VIN here to see if there is an open campaign:https://web.audiusa.com/recall/#Audi Recall Service Campaign Lookup

Fault Code: P3053
Standard OBD-II Code: P3053

--- Special Notes ---
• When found in MQB Golf based models such as the 2018 - 2019 (CDVC) 3.6L Vr6 VW Atlas verify the fuse to T91a/88 terminal of the ECM (623) labeled "SIG" in the wiring diagram is OK.  A blown or missing fused power supply to that terminal of the ECM will result in this DTC.  This was fuse SB22 on the 2019 Atlas we received feedback from.Wiring diagrams can be obtained from erWin directly:Official Factory Repair Information.
• Wiring diagrams can be obtained from erWin directly:Official Factory Repair Information.
• Wiring diagrams can be obtained from erWin directly:Official Factory Repair Information.

Fault Code: P307A/012410

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• MIL on
• Erratic idle
• Poor engine performance under load

--- Possible Causes ---
• Dirty Throttle Body
• Defective Throttle Body

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Clean Throttle Body and performThrottle Body Alignment (TBA)
• Replace Throttle Body and performThrottle Body Alignment (TBA)

--- Special Notes ---
• When found with DTC16452/P0068/000104, clean the Throttle Body and performThrottle Body Alignment (TBA).

Fault Code: P310B/012555

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• MIL On
• Reduced power

--- Possible Causes ---
• Faulty Fuel Pressure Sensor (G410)
• Wiring Harness for Fuel Pressure Sensor (G410)
• Low Fuel PressureWeak Lift PumpRestriction in Fuel Filter or Fuel Lines
• Weak Lift Pump
• Restriction in Fuel Filter or Fuel Lines
• Mechanical issue with High Pressure Fuel Pump
• Replacement fuel filter not correct Bar rating (ex. 4.0 Bar vs 6.0 Bar)
• Weak Lift Pump
• Restriction in Fuel Filter or Fuel Lines

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Replace Fuel Pressure Sensor (G410)
• Repair Damaged Wiring
• Remove and inspect High Pressure Fuel Pump
• Check Pressure Limiting Valve installed on the high pressure fuel rail when applicable

--- Special Notes ---
• When found inVW"FSI" Direct Injection engines, may also have DTCP129F/004767.  Can be caused by a faulty G410 sensor.  See TSB 01-07-69 or 01-11-11  (TPI 2016182) for more information on testing and updated part numbers.
• When found inVW"FSI" Direct Injection engines, may also have DTCs P129F, P008B, P129E, P008A, P2540 and/or P0087. See TSB 01-11-28 (TPI  2027458) for information which is similar to the first noted TSB.https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2012/SB-10044939-4844.pdfNHTSA
• https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2012/SB-10044939-4844.pdfNHTSA
• When found inVW"FSI" Direct Injection engines (3.6L CDVC), may also have DTC P008B.  See TT 20-18-06TT (TPI 2053320) for information on loose pins at G410 or G410 replacement.https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2018/MC-10154081-9999.pdfNHTSA
• https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2018/MC-10154081-9999.pdfNHTSA
• The G410 Fuel Pressure Sensor has a VW Warranty Extension in the US market due to premature failure.  Regardless of the vehicle brand, contact the local dealer with the full VIN to see if warranty extensions, recalls or RVU (required vehicle updates) apply.
• When found inAudi2.0T "FSI" Direct Injection engines, may also have DTCsP129F/004767, P2293 and/or P0087.   See TSB 24-08-58  (TPI 2017356/2) for more information on testing and updated part numbers.
• When found on Direct Injection engines, such as the 2.0T, in combination with system too lean or similar faults indicating a vacuum leak address the cause of those first. Common fault codes are foundhere
• When this fault is stored after all of the typical failure items have been replaced or tested, verify the electric fuel pump and/or FPCM (Fuel Pump Control Module) is operating properly.  Low fuel pressure or volume may be the end result of a fuel supply problem including an incorrect fuel filter when the pressure regulator is integral.When found on FSI engines, MVB 106.2 shows the duty cycle of the lift pump, this value should be below 70%.  The higher the number, the more the lift pump has to run to maintain fuel pressure to the High Pressure Fuel Pump.This video may be helpful:
• When found on FSI engines, MVB 106.2 shows the duty cycle of the lift pump, this value should be below 70%.  The higher the number, the more the lift pump has to run to maintain fuel pressure to the High Pressure Fuel Pump.
• This video may be helpful:
• https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2012/SB-10044939-4844.pdfNHTSA
• https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2018/MC-10154081-9999.pdfNHTSA
• When found on FSI engines, MVB 106.2 shows the duty cycle of the lift pump, this value should be below 70%.  The higher the number, the more the lift pump has to run to maintain fuel pressure to the High Pressure Fuel Pump.
• This video may be helpful:
http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jThQVzYFbVo
• We have had several reports where the intank Lift Pump was replaced with an "OE" unit from a 3rd party supplier and still have issues.  Our suggestion is to purchase a proper OE unit from the dealer's parts department.

Fault Code: P310C/012556

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) active

--- Possible Causes ---
• Fuel Reservoir empty
• Fuel Filter clocked
• Fuel Pump (G6) faulty

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Check Fuel Level
• Check Fuel Filter
• Check Fuel Pump (G6)

Fault Code: P320C/012812

--- Possible Causes ---
• Exhaust Leak before O2 Sensor
• False air leak in the intake system after the MAF sensor
• Failed O2 Sensors

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Repair exhaust Leak before O2 Sensor
• Repair false air leak in the intake system after the MAF sensor
• Replace failed O2 Sensors
• Reset O2 Sensor adapted values, see notes below

--- Special Notes ---
• When found in NAR 2009 - 2010 Volkswagen Jetta with a 2.0L TDI CBEA or CJAA, a new ECM software version is available, please see:TSB 23-11-01TPI 2025546Required Vehicle Update – 23H1
• TSB 23-11-01
• TPI 2025546
• Required Vehicle Update – 23H1
• TSB 23-11-01
• TPI 2025546
• Required Vehicle Update – 23H1
• Check Measuring Value Blocks (CBEA/CJAA) Group 041-(B1S1) and 136-(B1S2) for O2 Sensor adapted valuesNormal Range is [-1.28 to 1.22] - if out of range suspect failed O2 SensorsTo reset the above value after a repair, disconnect the O2 sensors, cycle the ignition on/off for 5 seconds and reconnect O2 sensors, clear codes and check MVB data again.
• Normal Range is [-1.28 to 1.22] - if out of range suspect failed O2 SensorsTo reset the above value after a repair, disconnect the O2 sensors, cycle the ignition on/off for 5 seconds and reconnect O2 sensors, clear codes and check MVB data again.
• To reset the above value after a repair, disconnect the O2 sensors, cycle the ignition on/off for 5 seconds and reconnect O2 sensors, clear codes and check MVB data again.
• Normal Range is [-1.28 to 1.22] - if out of range suspect failed O2 SensorsTo reset the above value after a repair, disconnect the O2 sensors, cycle the ignition on/off for 5 seconds and reconnect O2 sensors, clear codes and check MVB data again.
• To reset the above value after a repair, disconnect the O2 sensors, cycle the ignition on/off for 5 seconds and reconnect O2 sensors, clear codes and check MVB data again.
• To reset the above value after a repair, disconnect the O2 sensors, cycle the ignition on/off for 5 seconds and reconnect O2 sensors, clear codes and check MVB data again.

Fault Code: P334B/013131

--- Possible Symptoms ---
• Malfunction Indicator Light (MIL) and/or Exhaust Warning Lamp active
• Reduced Idle quality and Engine Performance

--- Possible Causes ---
• Faulty Charge Pressure Control Actuator
• Faulty Turbocharger
• Wiring issues to/from Charge Pressure Control Actuator
• Mechanical adjustment problem with Charge Pressure Control Actuator and Turbocharger.Engine Controller software issue with 1.2l TFSI engines.See Special Notes
• Engine Controller software issue with 1.2l TFSI engines.See Special Notes
• Engine Controller software issue with 1.2l TFSI engines.See Special Notes

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Check Charge Pressure Control Actuator and Turbocharger
• Check wiring to/from Charge Pressure Control Actuator
• Check the Mechanical adjustment of the Charge Pressure Control Actuator and TurbochargerCheck TPIs for known Engine Controller software issues with 1.2l TFSI engines.See Special Notes
• Check TPIs for known Engine Controller software issues with 1.2l TFSI engines.See Special Notes
• Check TPIs for known Engine Controller software issues with 1.2l TFSI engines.See Special Notes

--- Special Notes ---
• When found in 1.2l TFSI engines check theOfficial Factory Repair Informationfor TPIs that cover known issues with this DTC in addition toP334C.  The following TPIs may be helpful:Audi TPI# 2027809/9VW Commercial (i.e. VW Caddy) TPI# 2031930/1VW TPI# 2027485/7
• Audi TPI# 2027809/9
• VW Commercial (i.e. VW Caddy) TPI# 2031930/1
• VW TPI# 2027485/7
• When found in the 1.4T engines after wastegate actuator or turbo replacement, this forum post may be helpful:https://forums.ross-tech.com/showthread.php?23664-Case-Study-1-4T-CZTA-P056E-and-P334B-after-new-turbo-install
• https://forums.ross-tech.com/showthread.php?23664-Case-Study-1-4T-CZTA-P056E-and-P334B-after-new-turbo-install
• Audi TPI# 2027809/9

• VW TPI# 2027485/7

Fault Code: U102F/053295

--- Possible Causes ---
• Wiring, connections concerns or faulty G611-Radiator Identification Sensor

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Correct wiring, connection concerns or replace failed G611-Radiator Identification Sensor (w/radiator).

--- Special Notes ---
• Currently, the G611-Radiator Identification Sensor is not available separately, it comes with a new radiator when ordered from the vehicle's manufacture. The sensor will need to be attached to the radiator with AMV-195-KD1-01 adhesive, See Volkswagen Tech Tip 19-12-01 for more information.https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2015/MC-10122979-9999.pdfNHTSA
• https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2015/MC-10122979-9999.pdfNHTSA
• https://static.nhtsa.gov/odi/tsbs/2015/MC-10122979-9999.pdfNHTSA
• The cooling surface of the radiator on some Volkswagen/Audi Group vehicles is coated with a catalyst that helps reduce Ozone, The G611 sensor monitors this function and is part of the federal emissions control of these vehicles which allows the manufacture to receive EPA 'credits'.This technology is called PremAir®, see the BASF web site for more information.https://catalysts.basf.com/products-and-industries/mobile-emissions-catalysts/specialized-catalysts/premair
• This technology is called PremAir®, see the BASF web site for more information.
• https://catalysts.basf.com/products-and-industries/mobile-emissions-catalysts/specialized-catalysts/premair
• This technology is called PremAir®, see the BASF web site for more information.
• https://catalysts.basf.com/products-and-industries/mobile-emissions-catalysts/specialized-catalysts/premair
• Note: The fault code 'U1030-LIN bus inactive' may also be present,this should clear when the U102F fault has been corrected.

Fault Code: U1052/053330

--- Possible Causes ---
• Faulty wiring/connections
• Faulty Generator/Alternator

--- Possible Solutions ---
• Correct wiring, connection concerns
• Replace faulty Generator/Alternator

--- Special Notes ---
• When found on vehicles with 'Coupling Point-TML', (8-Pin connector), check for possible wiring damage. 'Coupling Point-TML' can be located near left frame rail, below left headlight.
Wire damage found on a 2018 NAR Tiguan (Images Courtesy of: J&M Automotive)
• LIN-Bus wire for Generator(purple/white)
• TML - Coupling Point (T8)(8 - pin connector)
